modules.title: Agent engineering with AI
modules.subtitle: Tools, prompts, systems – design and execution
modules.description: Agent engineering: cycle, tools, prompts, integrations. AI as agent system builder.

slides.title: Agent engineering path
slides.subtitle: Tools, prompts, systems

content.whyBenefit: After this module you will understand how to design AI agents – from the cycle, tools and context to integrations.
content.heroStat: You already know the 6 blocks and context engineering.
content.heroSubText: Design agents for your processes – taxonomy, multi-agent coordination, no-code tools. Here you apply the context engineering from Modules 4–6 to agents.
content.firstActionCTA: Continue to the first practice task: on the “Agent engineering path” slide you will check in 60 seconds how an AI agent differs from a simple question–answer.
outcomes: Understand agent taxonomy (L0–L3) and multi-agent roles
Use 5 workflow patterns and context rules for agents
Know how to choose tools and limit risks
content.audience: For business specialists and engineers who completed Modules 4–6 (Context engineering).

slides.title: Agent engineering path – what you will find
slides.subtitle: Short overview; detail on later slides

sections.heading: In short
sections.body:
In this module, in order: **agent cycle** (diagram), **3A strategy**, **AI agent types and roles**, **5 workflow patterns**, **role and system prompt**, **AI tool selection**, **when to choose an agent**, **errors and limits**, **workflow concepts** (trigger, condition, action, webhook), **business automation platforms** (Zapier, Make, n8n, Power Automate). Optional: deeper spec and testing; glossary; summary.

We **do not repeat** term definitions here – find them on the **Key concepts** slide.

sections.heading: Micro-win in 60 seconds
sections.body: Before deeper theory, do one small check: can you already distinguish an agent from a simple question–answer?

sections.heading: Copyable prompt
sections.body: Copy into AI and adapt the example to your work.
sections.copyable: Explain in one sentence how an AI agent differs from a simple question–answer. Give one example from my work where I would need an agent, not one simple prompt.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: If the answer has no steps or tools, it is still a simple prompt. On the next slide you will see the full agent cycle.

slides.title: Agent cycle and architecture
slides.subtitle: Agent → Planning → Tools → Result → Feedback

sections.heading: Process diagram
sections.body: Agent cycle: from task to result and feedback. View full size – the same image opens in a modal.

sections.heading: How an agent works
sections.body:
An AI agent **(1)** receives a task, **(2)** plans steps, **(3)** uses tools (search, API, files – if the platform allows), **(4)** returns a result with feedback.

**Cycle in practice:** understand → choose tool → execute → based on the result, decide whether to repeat the step or finish. Unlike a simple chat – the agent **autonomously** chooses actions and can call external tools.

sections.heading: When to use an agent (brief)
sections.body:
- You need **several steps**, not one answer.
- Hard to describe everything with **pure rules**.
- There is **unstructured data** (text, emails, feedback) that must be interpreted.

A **simple prompt** is enough when one clear question and one answer – no search or external systems.

**More detail** – with examples and an agent template – on **When to choose an agent vs a simple prompt**.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Copy the task below into AI and run it.

sections.heading: Copyable AI task
sections.body: Task for AI (paste into chat): “Explain in your own words **in four steps** how an AI agent works, and **in one sentence** – how it differs from one question and one answer without tools.”
sections.copyable: Explain in your own words in four steps how an AI agent works, and in one sentence – how it differs from one question and one answer without tools.

slides.title: 3A strategy
slides.subtitle: Automatize 80 % / Augment 15 % / Autonomize 5 %

sections.heading: 3A strategy (diagram)
sections.body: Visually: the largest share – **Automatize (80 %)**, smaller – **Augment (15 %)**, smallest – **Autonomize (5 %)**. Balance of value and safety. **View full size** – same image in a modal.

sections.heading: Three bands (80 / 15 / 5)
sections.body:
**AUTOMATIZE (80 %)** – rule-based flows, no human decision. E.g.: form → CRM → email → Slack.

**AUGMENT (15 %)** – human decides, AI helps (summaries, classification, recommendations). E.g.: email → AI summary → human approval → send.

**AUTONOMIZE (5 %)** – AI agents with limits: RAG, quality control, escalation. E.g.: feedback → sentiment AI → escalation → ticket.

sections.heading: Where to apply
sections.body: When planning business automation: which processes need only rules (80), which need human approval (15), where an agent can act with limits (5). The 80/15/5 rule – optimal value and safety ratio.

slides.title: AI agent types and roles
slides.subtitle: Depth levels L0–L3 and multi-agent roles for business

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: Not every task needs an AI team. First set the **depth level** (L0–L3), then whether you need several **roles** (coordinator, specialist, evaluator, router).

sections.heading: Taxonomy diagram
sections.body: Left – depth levels (L0–L3); right – multi-agent roles and handoffs. **View full size** – same image in a modal.

sections.heading: Depth levels (L0–L3)
sections.body:
**L0 – Chat with AI:** one question–answer (e.g. drafting an email).

**L1 – Single AI agent:** several steps + tools (research + summary).

**L2 – AI team:** different roles, handoffs (RFP: researcher → writer → reviewer).

**L3 – Automated flow:** trigger → actions (form → CRM → email).

sections.heading: Multi-agent roles
sections.body:
**Coordinator** – breaks down the task, delegates, merges results (team lead).

**Specialist** – does one narrow job: search, draft, calculation.

**Evaluator** – QC, rules, returns for revision (editor / compliance).

**Router** – routes by type (reception / triage).

sections.heading: 3A mapping
sections.body: **Automatize (80 %)** → usually L3 (flow, no multi-agent). **Augment (15 %)** → L1 + human (HITL). **Autonomize (5 %)** → L2 with evaluator and incident playbook.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Pick one work process and define three roles – coordinator, specialist, evaluator.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt
sections.body: Copy and fill [X] with your process.
sections.copyable:
Define three roles for your process [X]:
1) Coordinator – what they plan and delegate
2) Specialist – what they do specifically
3) Evaluator – what they check before delivering the result
For each role – one sentence + input + output.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Does each role have a clear **input** and **output** description? If not – too complex; start with L1 (one agent).

sections.heading: When NOT to use multi-agent
sections.body: One agent is enough for a simple task. Multi-agent too early if there is no HITL or clear “done” criterion. Start simple – add roles only when one agent is no longer enough.

slides.title: 5 workflow patterns for business
slides.subtitle: Chain, routing, parallel, coordinator, evaluator

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: When one AI agent is no longer enough, use **workflow patterns** – not programming, but a clear plan of roles and handoffs.

sections.heading: 5 patterns
sections.body:
**1. Chain** – one step after another (query → classification → draft → send).

**2. Routing** – different branch by type (complaint / inquiry / proposal).

**3. Parallel work** – several specialists at once, then merge (CRM + email → one summary).

**4. Coordinator + specialists** – dynamically assigns sub-tasks (weekly report: data + trends → 1 page).

**5. Generator + evaluator** – draft → QC → fix (email, report, FAQ).

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Pick one pattern for your process and note which roles perform each step.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt (coordinator)
sections.body: Copy and fill [DESCRIBE].
sections.copyable:
You are the coordinator. Task: [DESCRIBE].
Break into 2–3 sub-tasks. For each assign a role (specialist / evaluator),
input, output and handoff rule (when to pass to the next role).
Present the plan as a numbered list.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Does the plan have a clear **completion criterion**? Do risky steps have **HITL** (human approval)?

sections.heading: When NOT to use multi-agent
sections.body: If the task has 1–2 steps – L1 (one agent) is enough. Multi-agent without an evaluator is risky for finance, support and public content.

content.footer: Next – slide 9: Section – multi-agent and workflow

slides.title: Section: Multi-agent → workflow
slides.subtitle: 5 patterns completed – next, agent design in practice.

content.title: Section: Multi-agent → workflow
content.subtitle: Next: closed learning loop, role, tools, and error handling.
content.celebrationText: Great! Taxonomy and 5 workflow patterns are now in your toolkit.
nextSteps: Closed learning loop – how errors become rules and skills.
Role and system prompt – how to set agent limits.
When to use an agent and when to use a simple prompt.

recap.heading: What do you already know?
recap.lead: The agent engineering path now has a multi-agent foundation.
items: L0–L3 taxonomy – when one agent is enough and when you need several.
Coordinator, specialist, evaluator, router – clear roles.
5 workflow patterns – chain, routing, parallel work, and more.

slides.title: Closed learning loop
slides.subtitle: Rules, skills, logs, evaluation and lessons

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: An agent does not only execute a task. A good system records what happened, evaluates the result, extracts lessons from errors and updates rules or skills for the next run.

sections.heading: Two system layers
sections.body:
**Execution system:** task → rules → coordinator → specialists / evaluator → skills → output.

**Learning loop:** logs → evaluation → lessons → update. This turns one failed run into a better next run.

sections.heading: Blocks in plain language
sections.body:
**Rules** – limits and principles: what AI does not do, when it asks a human.

**Skills** – reusable capabilities: search, writing, checking, or one Skill package with instructions.

**Logs** – task, steps, errors, human feedback.

**Evaluation** – test or review: did the result match criteria.

**Lessons** – short note: what failed, what to change, where to update a rule or skill.

**Update** – changed rule, prompt, skill description or test for next time.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: After one small scenario, write one lesson: what failed, which rule or skill fixes it, where that fix will be used next time.

sections.heading: Copyable lesson template
sections.body: Use after a Module 12 scenario or any agent trial.
sections.copyable:
Lesson after agent run:
1) What happened?
2) What was the cause?
3) What do I update: rule, prompt, skill or test?
4) How will I check it next time?

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: A good lesson changes the next run. If you only write “failed” but do not update a rule, skill or test, the learning loop is not closed.

content.footer: Next – slide 11: Role and system prompt

slides.title: Role and system prompt template
slides.subtitle: How to set role and limits for an agent

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: Role defines how AI behaves – in simple chat and in agent systems. The system prompt is part of context engineering: here you define role, limits, tools and what to do when data is missing.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Copy the prompt below into your AI tool’s “system settings” field (if available). Then ask one agent-style query.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt
sections.body: Below – system prompt you can copy into AI tool settings.
sections.copyable:
Your role – assistant that completes tasks step by step.
Use tools (search, calculators, etc.) when they help the answer.
If the task is unclear or fails – briefly say why and what to fix.
Do not invent data – if facts are needed, use search or write “I don’t know”.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: If AI does not use tools when needed – check whether the platform has agent features enabled (Browse, Tools). If the answer has no sources – ask “provide sources” or specify sources in the input.

slides.title: Tool selection and limits
slides.subtitle: What AI can use – search, files, API; platforms (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini)

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: Not all AI tools have the same tools. In the task clearly write: “Use search and provide sources”. Tell the user (or yourself) what is available on the chosen platform.

sections.heading: Popular platforms – what they offer
sections.body: **ChatGPT** (OpenAI): web search (Browse), image generation (DALL·E), calculator. **Claude** (Anthropic): Tools – can connect search, API, custom functions. **Gemini** (Google): search, Workspace integration (Docs, Sheets, Gmail). Each platform differs – before writing an agent prompt check settings: is search or tools enabled.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Open your AI tool settings and note which tools you allow (search, files, API). Then in one task clearly write: “Use search and provide sources”.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt
sections.body: Below – prompt with search instruction; copy and fill [DESCRIBE TASK].
sections.copyable:
Task: [DESCRIBE TASK].
Use search to find the latest information. If you find sources – list them at the end of the answer. If not – write “Not found” and briefly explain what you searched for.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: If the answer has no links – did you say “use search”? Is the tool enabled on the platform?

sections.heading: Tool selection tree (interactive diagram)
sections.body: Pick a branch in the diagram – confirmation at the bottom. Full description – in **Business automation tools**, §21.

sections.heading: Tool selection tree (for workflow tools)
sections.body: **Office 365 heavy** (Teams, Outlook, SharePoint daily) → **Power Automate**. **Non-tech team, quick start** → **Zapier**. **Complex logic + good price** → **Make.com**. **Full control, self-host, data on our side** → **n8n**. **Enterprise governance, auditors, compliance** → **Workato**. Full comparison – in “Business automation tools”, §21.

slides.title: When to choose an agent vs a simple prompt
slides.subtitle: Complex tasks with tools – agent; one question – simple prompt

sections.heading: In short
sections.body:
Agent – when the task is complex (several actions, external data, tools). Simple prompt – when one question, one answer, no external tools.

This continues the context engineering from Modules 4–6: before execution, decide what information, tools and limits AI needs.

**Multi-agent patterns** (chain, routing, etc.) – slide **10.48**.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt (example)
sections.body: Below – agent task example; fill [ROLE] and [X].
sections.copyable: Role: [ROLE]. Task: (1) Search [X], (2) pick 3–5 key sources, (3) write a summary in English with links. If not found – write “Not found” and why.

sections.heading: Agent prompt template (5 parts)
sections.body: A clear agent prompt has 5 parts: (1) Role – who you are, limits; (2) Task in steps – what to do in order; (3) Tools – what to use (search, API); (4) Output format – table, language, sources; (5) Error handling – what to return when it fails. Longer full template – expandable block below.

sections.heading: Full template – copy
sections.body: Use this template for any agent task. Fill the [ ] fields.
sections.copyable:
ROLE
You are [domain / role]. Your responsibility – [what you do]. You have access to [search / calculator / API]. Do not: [limits, e.g. do not invent data].

TASK (step by step)
(1) [First step, e.g. search X]
(2) [Second step, e.g. pick 3–5 sources]
(3) [Third step, e.g. write summary]

TOOLS
Use [search / tool] when [when]. If facts needed – use search or write “I don’t know”. List sources at the end.

FORMAT
[Table / list / report]. Language: English. Sources: cite them.

ERROR HANDLING
If the task cannot be completed (missing data, timeout): write “Failed: [reason]” and suggest what to fix. Never return an empty answer.

sections.heading: Rules and tips
sections.body: Rules: be clear (steps, not just goal); specify tools (“use search”, “provide sources”); error handling required; system prompt ideally up to ~1000 tokens; state limits (what not to do). Tips: before writing list 3–5 steps; if possible add 1 example (few-shot); test when data is missing – does AI return clear “Not found” / “Failed”.

sections.heading: Workflow specification (1 page)
sections.body: When an agent connects to a form, CRM, email or another tool, write a one-page specification before launch. Full testing and security – on the optional “Workflow, testing and security” slide.
sections.copyable:
Workflow specification:
1) Trigger – what starts the flow.
2) Input schema – which fields are required.
3) Condition – which condition changes the path.
4) Actions – 3–5 actions with tools.
5) Output – what result must be created.
6) Error handling – what to do if a step fails.
7) Audit log – what to record: time, step, result.
8) HITL – where human approval is required.

slides.title: Error handling and limits
slides.subtitle: What to do when something fails

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: The agent must know what to do when something fails. Set limits: what not to do, what to return instead of an empty answer.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Paste the text below into **system** or **user** prompt (or both). Then ask AI a deliberately impossible task – e.g. “Search for the 2029 profit report of non-existent company X and provide real sources”.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt
sections.body: Add to system or user prompt – what to do when it fails.
sections.copyable: If the task cannot be completed (missing data, disallowed action or timeout): write a short message “Failed: [reason]” and suggest what the user can fix. Never return an empty answer.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Did AI return **“Failed”** (or similar) and **reason**, not an empty answer? If it stays silent – add to prompt: “If you cannot – always say why.”

slides.title: Key concepts: trigger, action, condition, webhook
slides.subtitle: Workflow structure and examples

sections.heading: Workflow chain (diagram)
sections.body: Main sequence: **Trigger → Condition → Action**. **Webhook** – event from another system; often **starts** the same type of flow as a trigger (e.g. payment). **View full size** – same image in a modal.

sections.heading: Concepts and examples
sections.body:
**Trigger** – event that starts the workflow (new email, form submission, file in folder, payment).

**Condition** – rule for the next step (e.g. if value > €500 – send to manager; if email valid – write to CRM).

**Action** – action in a system (email, CRM entry, task, table update).

**Webhook** – real-time data transfer: event → call to another system’s URL with data (e.g. PayPal payment → webhook → order update).

**Integration** – how systems connect (Zapier/Make, API, file exchange) so data is not copied by hand.

**API** – agreed way for programs to exchange data over the network. AI agents often call APIs as tools.

**Polling** – periodic check “are there new data yet?” (different from webhook).

**Error handling (workflow)** – what to do when a step fails: retry, notify human, log, stop flow.

**Logs** – what ran, when, success or error (for debugging and audit; more – optional slide on spec and testing).

**Example 1:** form (trigger) → email check (condition) → CRM (action) → email (action) → Slack to team (action).

**Example 2:** payment (webhook) → order update → confirmation to customer.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Write down **one real trigger** from your work (e.g. “new lead in form”) and **two actions** you would want to run automatically next.

slides.title: Section: Workflow → automation
slides.subtitle: Trigger, action, webhook – completed. Next: platforms and testing.

content.title: Section: Workflow → automation
content.subtitle: Next: Zapier, Make, n8n, Power Automate, and optional spec/testing.
content.celebrationText: Good! The workflow chain and key concepts are clear.
nextSteps: Business automation tools – when to choose Zapier, Make, n8n, or Power Automate.
Optional: workflow spec, testing, and security.

recap.heading: What do you already know?
recap.lead: Agent cycle and prompts now have a workflow layer.
items: Trigger → Condition → Action – the core sequence.
Webhook – real-time data between systems.
Integration, API, error handling, and logs – for audit and debugging.

slides.title: Business automation tools
slides.subtitle: Zapier, Make, n8n, Power Automate – when to choose what

sections.heading: Zapier
sections.body: 7000+ integrations (Gmail, Slack, Salesforce, Google Workspace). No-code, quick start – fits small teams. **Example:** new meeting (e.g. Calendly) → calendar entry → reminder email to participant. **Strengths:** large integration library, low learning curve. **Weaknesses:** expensive at scale, limited error handling.

sections.heading: Make.com
sections.body: Visual builder (drag & drop), more complex logic than Zapier – fits mid-size business. **Example:** Shopify order → stock sheet → if stock low → email supplier. **Strengths:** conditions, loops, data transforms; better free tier. **Weaknesses:** learning curve, many scenarios harder to manage.

sections.heading: n8n
sections.body: Open-source, full control, data on your servers. Fits technical teams. **Example:** payment platform webhook → validation and DB entry → on error notify alert channel (Slack). **Strengths:** unlimited workflows, custom code, advanced error handling. **Weaknesses:** IT skills, server administration.

sections.heading: Power Automate
sections.body: Microsoft ecosystem – Excel, Teams, Outlook, SharePoint, Office 365. Fits admin, sales, project management. **Example:** Outlook + PDF → SharePoint → Teams → data extraction → Excel. **Strengths:** enterprise security, integrated licensing. **Weaknesses:** limited outside Microsoft ecosystem.

slides.title: Workflow, testing and security (optional)
slides.subtitle: Specification, 10 test scenarios, security, tool tree – visible content

sections.heading: Why here?
sections.body: When you build **workflows between systems** (Zapier, Make, n8n) or agents with external data – you need consistent specification, testing and security rules. Below – full visible content; more detail – in **Business automation tools** (docs/AUTOMATIZAVIMO_IRANKIAI_VERSLUI.md).

sections.heading: Workflow specification (diagram)
sections.body: **8 spec blocks** as one sequence: from trigger to audit log. More text – section below. **View full size** – same image in a modal.

sections.heading: Incident playbook (diagram)
sections.body: **5 incident steps** for security: stop, log, assess scope, notify and fix. More text – security section below. **View full size** – same image in a modal.

sections.heading: Workflow specification (8 blocks)
sections.body: **Trigger** – what starts it (event, frequency). **Input schema** – fields, formats, required/optional. **Condition** – rules (e.g. if field X > 500). **Actions** – step list with tools. **Output** – what we get (CRM entry, email, ticket). **SLA, retries, rate limits** – max wait, retry count, API limits. **Error handling** – on failure (retry, alert, human escalation). **Audit log** – what we record (run_id, time, step, result).

sections.heading: 10 test scenarios (before going live)
sections.body:
(1) Empty fields (required empty). (2) Invalid email format. (3) Duplicates – same record twice. (4) Timeout – API does not respond in time. (5) Webhook duplicates – same webhook twice. (6) Special characters / long text in fields. (7) Invalid values (non-number where number expected). (8) Missing fields (schema changed). (9) Rate limit hit (429). (10) Auth error (401/403).

**Idempotency (simply):** rules so **the same action does not run twice** from repeated webhook or double click – e.g. unique request ID; if record with that ID exists – **update**, do not create new.

sections.heading: 3 AI agent QC tests (additionally)
sections.body: Before launch check the AI agent: (1) empty input – does it return clear “Failed”? (2) false fact – does it not confirm? (3) very long text – does it not break without notice?

sections.heading: Security and compliance
sections.body: **PII:** What may be sent to AI, what to mask (name, email – per GDPR and company policy). **Access:** Who can edit workflow (viewer, editor, admin); API keys – not in code, use secrets manager or platform vault. **Incident playbook (5 steps):** (1) Stop workflow / disconnect integration. (2) Record – logs, who, when, what. (3) Assess scope. (4) Notify – DPO, manager, authorities if needed. (5) Fix and deploy safeguards. **Human-in-the-loop:** When human approval is mandatory – e.g. financial ops above amount X, bulk personal data export.

sections.heading: Tool selection tree
sections.body: We **do not repeat** the table here – same tree and interactive diagram on **Tool selection and limits (10.4)**. More – in **Business automation tools** (§21).

sections.heading: Full guide
sections.body: All material – **Business automation tools** (docs/AUTOMATIZAVIMO_IRANKIAI_VERSLUI.md): §18 spec, §19 testing, §20 security, §21 tool tree and more.

slides.title: Glossary (optional)
slides.subtitle: Agent engineering terms – want more detail?

terms.definition: System that completes tasks in steps and can use tools (search, API, files).
Function AI can call (e.g. search, calculator, file read).
Setting “who you are” and “how to behave” – visible to AI, not the user.
Connection between AI and external data or services (API, database).
Step completion – planning and tool calls.
Rules the agent does not break (e.g. disallowed actions, privacy).
What to return to the user when the task fails.
Event that starts a workflow (e.g. new email, form submission).
Action the system performs (e.g. send email, write to CRM).
Rule – when to run the next step (e.g. if value > €500).
Real-time data transfer between systems (event → API call).
Multi-agent role – breaks down task, delegates to specialists, merges results.
Multi-agent role – checks quality and rules; can return for revision (QC).
Multi-agent role – routes the request to the right role or flow by type.
Cycle: understand → choose tool → execute → observe result → repeat or finish.

slides.title: Module 10 summary
slides.subtitle: What you learned – agent cycle, tools, prompts

content.introBody: Congratulations! Key wins: agent taxonomy (L0–L3, roles), 5 workflow patterns, 3A, closed learning loop, AI and no-code tools, prompts and error handling.

sections.heading: Taxonomy and multi-agent
items: L0–L3 depth levels
Coordinator, specialist, evaluator, router
5 workflow patterns for business

sections.heading: 3A, learning loop and safety
items: Automatize 80 % / Augment 15 % / Autonomize 5 %
Closed learning loop: logs → evaluation → lessons → update
HITL, error handling and AI agent QC tests

sections.heading: Prompts and use case catalogue
items: 5-part agent template
Sales: lead→CRM; pipeline; RFP
People, finance and support: from summary to escalation

content.reflectionPrompt:
META: You are a training reflection assistant. Goal – consolidate what you learned about AI agents.
INPUT: I just finished training on AI agents – taxonomy, multi-agent, workflow, closed learning loop, prompts.
OUTPUT: Ask 3 questions: (1) Which agent scenario could I apply today? (2) Which lesson after a run would I want to record? (3) What do I want to try first? After my answers, give one concrete tip.
content.tagline: Agent = steps + tools + limits – your foundation for automation.
content.nextStepCTA: Go to Module 11: Knowledge check (Agent path)
content.firstAction24h: Today open one AI tool with tools enabled (search or Tools) and ask one agent query with “Use search and provide sources”.

slides.title: Checkpoint: agent cycle
slides.subtitle: Agent engineering path – step 1

content.title: Agent cycle – I ran 1 agent-style query
content.body: Run a short agent-style query with search or a tool. Mark this step when the answer shows not only text, but clear steps, sources, or a tool trace.

sections.heading: What to check
sections.body: Does the answer have at least 2 steps? Does it say which tool AI used or why it did not use one?
sections.copyable: Task: [DESCRIBE]. Use search or a tool if needed. Return the answer in 3 steps: what you did, which tool you used, what the result was. If you did not use a tool, explain why.

sections.heading: If it failed
sections.body: Add: “use search”, “include sources”, “if you cannot find it – say Not found”.

slides.title: Checkpoint: roles and handoff
slides.subtitle: Agent engineering path – step 2

content.title: Roles – I defined inputs and outputs
content.body: Pick one process and write 3 roles: coordinator, specialist, evaluator. Mark this step when each role has a clear input and output.

sections.heading: What to check
sections.body: Is it clear when the specialist passes work to the evaluator? Does the evaluator have a completion criterion?
sections.copyable: Process: [DESCRIBE]. Define 3 roles: coordinator, specialist, evaluator. For each role, specify: input, output and handoff rule – when work is passed to the next role.

sections.heading: If too complex
sections.body: Reduce to L1: one agent, one tool, one result.

slides.title: Checkpoint: agent prompt
slides.subtitle: Agent engineering path – step 3

content.title: Agent prompt – I have a template before practice
content.body: Before Module 12 practice, have one 5-part agent prompt, a draft one-page workflow specification and 1 golden check (edge case). This will be your start for the Coordinator + 2 specialists scenario.

sections.heading: Minimum artefact
sections.body: Role, step-by-step task, tools, output format, error handling + Trigger, Actions, Output, HITL.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: If error handling or human approval is missing in a risky place, add it before continuing.

sections.heading: Golden check
sections.body: Test the prompt with 1 edge case: a required field is missing, source is not found, or API does not respond. Good result – AI returns clear “Failed: [reason]” and suggests what to fix.
sections.copyable: Test this agent prompt with an edge case: [PASTE PROMPT]. Test situation: [required field is missing / source is not found / API does not respond]. Evaluate whether the answer includes “Failed: [reason]” and a clear suggestion for what to fix.

modules.title: Knowledge check (Agent path)
modules.subtitle: Test: agent cycle, prompts, tools
modules.description: Check your understanding before the final Agent engineering project (Module 12).

slides.title: Module 11 test
slides.subtitle: Agent engineering knowledge

content.whyBenefit: After this test you will know if you are ready for the final Agent engineering project (Module 12).
content.firstActionCTA: Answer 9 questions – topics: agent cycle, taxonomy, multi-agent, workflow, 3A and the learning loop.
content.microWinPhrase: Each correct answer shows you are ready to design agents.
content.thresholdExplanation: When you reach ≥70 %, you can go to Module 12 (project). If less – we recommend reviewing Module 10 slides.

slides.title: Questions
slides.subtitle: Agent cycle, tools, prompts

testQuestions.question: Scenario: an AI agent gives a confident answer but does not cite sources, even though the task required current facts. What should you fix first?
options: Clearly specify the tool and rule: use search, cite sources, if not found – write “Not found”
Increase answer length to 2000 words
Remove the error-handling sentence
Keep the same prompt and just ask again
testQuestions.explanation: The agent needs clear context: which tool to use, when to cite sources, and what to return when sources are missing. This reduces invented facts.

testQuestions.question: Where are AI role and limits set in an agent system?
options: Only in user prompt
In system prompt
Only in tool list
Nowhere
testQuestions.explanation: System prompt sets role, principles and limits – visible to AI, not the user.

testQuestions.question: How does an evaluator differ from a specialist in multi-agent?
options: Evaluator checks quality; specialist does the work
Evaluator always sends emails; specialist only reads
They do the same, just with different tools
Evaluator sets the trigger
testQuestions.explanation: Specialist does narrow work (search, draft). Evaluator checks rules, quality and can return for revision – separate QC role.

testQuestions.question: What to do when the agent cannot complete the task?
options: Nothing – wait
Specify in system or user prompt: write “Failed” and reason
Turn off tools
Repeat the same prompt
testQuestions.explanation: Error handling – clearly state what to return (e.g. “Failed: [reason]” and what to fix).

testQuestions.question: What is a router in an AI team?
options: Role that routes the request to the right role by type
Tool that sends emails automatically
Same as coordinator – synonym
AI model version
testQuestions.explanation: Router classifies input and routes to the right specialist or flow – like reception or triage.

testQuestions.question: Scenario: you receive an RFP document and want: researcher gathers facts, writer prepares summary, evaluator checks criteria. Which workflow pattern fits best?
options: Coordinator + specialists (or chain with roles)
Only one prompt without role
Routing by email size
Parallel work without merging
testQuestions.explanation: RFP with several roles – typical L2 (AI team): coordinator plans, specialists execute, evaluator checks. Chain with roles also works.

testQuestions.question: Scenario: a client fills a form on the website; you want the new entry to go to Google Sheets and the client to get email confirmation. What should you do first when designing the flow (Zapier, Make, etc.)?
options: Define the trigger – what starts the flow (e.g. new form submission), then actions (Sheets, email)
First write only a long AI chat text with no link to form or email
Only pick Slack channel name and colour for the notification
Launch to production and then think about errors
testQuestions.explanation: First – event that starts the flow (trigger) and input schema; then actions (entry, email). Clear what starts the chain and what data moves.

testQuestions.question: Scenario: you get a long complaint by email. You want AI to briefly summarise it, but you write and send the final reply to the client only after you review it. Which option best matches Augment (15 %)?
options: Flow: email → AI summary → you review / edit → then send to client
AI automatically replies to client without your step
Do not use AI – do everything by hand
AI deletes the email if longer than 500 words
testQuestions.explanation: Augment – human stays in the decision, AI helps (summary, classification). Human-in-the-loop before sending reduces risk.

testQuestions.question: Scenario: an agent completed the task, but the result was inaccurate. After error analysis, you find the cause. What should you do so the learning loop closes, not just describes the problem?
options: Update a rule, prompt, skill or test based on the cause – not only write what happened
Write a note in a list and continue with the same prompt
Increase the agent answer length
Run the same request again without changes
testQuestions.explanation: The learning loop closes only when the lesson becomes a concrete update: rule, prompt, skill or test. Stating the fact alone does not close the loop.

slides.title: Results
slides.subtitle: Ready for Module 12?

content.passedMessage: Congratulations! You are ready for the Agent engineering project (Module 12).
content.failedMessage: We recommend reviewing Module 10 slides: 10.2 (cycle), 10.15 (workflow), 10.25 (3A), 10.45 (taxonomy), 10.48 (workflow patterns), 10.3–10.6 (prompts, tools, errors), 10.35 (Zapier/Make).
content.thresholdExplanation: When you reach ≥70 %, you can go to Module 12. Less – review slides 10.2, 10.15, 10.25, 10.45, 10.48 and the prompt block (10.3–10.6).

useCaseBlock.heading: Where to apply?
useCaseBlock.body: Taxonomy, multi-agent, workflow (trigger, action), 3A and tools – research, reports, support triage, RFP analysis.

slides.title: Bonus: agent pipeline in 5 min
slides.subtitle: Coordinator → specialist → evaluator

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: After the test, try a mini agent pipeline in 5 minutes without Zapier or Make: 3 separate AI chats, clear handoff and one quality check.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Pick a small process: RFP summary, weekly sales or customer complaint. Run the prompt set below and record what you pass from one role to the next.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt set
sections.body: Use in 3 separate AI chats.
sections.copyable:
COORDINATOR: Task: [DESCRIBE]. Split into 2 sub-tasks, assign a specialist and evaluator. Specify input, output and handoff rule.

SPECIALIST: Input: [COORDINATOR PLAN]. Do the assigned work. Output: short summary, facts, uncertainties.

EVALUATOR: Check the specialist output by criteria: facts, clarity, risk. If data is missing – return a list of fixes.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Do you have a clear handoff and 1 test case where data is missing? If yes, you are ready for the Module 12 prompt-first start (124.5).

modules.title: Final project (Agent path)
modules.subtitle: One full agent or automation scenario
modules.description: Build one agent or automation scenario – continuation of M10 and M11 (Agent path).

slides.title: Agent engineering project
slides.subtitle: Prompt-first start + 3 full practices with artefacts

content.whyBenefit: After the project you will have ready artefacts: a prompt-only multi-agent diagram or a full workflow (trigger, condition, actions), field mapping, test cases, log evidence and one Skill package for reuse. Quick start – Coordinator + 2 specialists (124.5); full delivery-first path – 3 practices per 3A.
content.firstActionCTA: Start with 124.5 Coordinator + 2 specialists if you want a prompt-only path; if you have an automation tool, go to Lab #1, #2 or #3 and complete it with artefacts.
content.recommendedStart: First start: Coordinator + 2 specialists (124.5). Full path: practices 1–3 (Automatize, Augment, Autonomize). Optional: Research agent and M10 recap scenarios.
content.primaryPathIntro: If you do not have Zapier / Make or want a quick start, first do Coordinator + 2 specialists (124.5): 3 separate AI chats, a diagram and 1 test case. This lowers the tool barrier and prepares you for the 3 full labs.
content.meaningParagraph:
This completes the Agent path. First, you can create a manual prompt pipeline without extra tools; then, if you have an automation platform, move to the 3 full labs with real artefacts.

Before choosing a process, estimate return on investment (ROI): (tasks per week × minutes per task × hourly cost) − tool cost. Fill 3 scenarios: now, after 3 months, after 12 months.
content.taskOneLiner: Start with 124.5 → create a manual multi-agent pipeline → then choose Lab #1, #2 or #3 for full delivery-first work.

roiTemplate.heading: Return on investment (ROI) mini calculator
roiTemplate.body: Before choosing a process, estimate value simply: (tasks per week × minutes per task × hourly cost) − tool cost. Fill 3 scenarios: now, after 3 months, after 12 months.
roiTemplate.copyable:
Return on investment (ROI) calculation:
Process: [e.g. RFP summary]
Tasks per week: [number]
Minutes per task now: [number]
Hourly cost: [€]
Tool and maintenance cost per month: [€]

Now: [saving]
After 3 months: [saving]
After 12 months: [saving]

Decision: automate / augment / keep manual?

slides.title: Three practices: Automatize, Augment, Autonomize
slides.subtitle: Diagram – where human approval is needed

sections.heading: Comparison
sections.body: Three labs – different automation levels. Green label – where human approval is usually needed.
sections.imageAlt: Three agent practices: Automatize, Augment, Autonomize

slides.title: Business multi-agent schema
slides.subtitle: Input → roles → evaluator → output (+ HITL)

content.whyBenefit: Understand when one AI is not enough and how to split work without coding. Practice – Coordinator + 2 specialists slide (124.5).

sections.heading: Business multi-agent schema
sections.body:
**1. Input** – task, data, constraints.

**2. Router** (optional) – classifies type, routes.

**3. Coordinator** – breaks down, assigns 2–3 specialists.

**4. Specialists** – narrow work (research, draft, calculation).

**5. Evaluator** – QC, rules, returns for revision.

**6. Output** – with **HITL gate** before sending to client or manager.

sections.heading: Handoff
sections.body: Passing to the next role – clear rule: “When specialist finishes X, pass to evaluator in Y format.” Without handoff multi-agent becomes chaotic.

slides.title: Practice 1: Automatize (80 %)
slides.subtitle: Form → Sheets/CRM → Email → Slack/Teams (Zapier or Make)

scenario.narrativeLead: Pick a form and destinations (Sheets/CRM, email, Slack or Teams). Build the workflow and record artefacts.
scenario.situation: Automatize (80 %): rule-based flows – form to CRM, email and team notification without manual decision at each step.
scenario.context: Build workflow: Form (Google Forms / Typeform) → Sheets or CRM → personalised email → Slack/Teams. Tool: Zapier or Make.
scenario.data: Rule-based flows. Task: build a working workflow and record artefacts – diagram, field mapping, tests, log evidence.
scenario.constraints: Use Zapier or Make (free tier is enough). Describe what to do if API does not respond (error handling).
scenario.expectedFormat: Artefacts: 1-page workflow spec (trigger, input schema, conditions, actions, output, error handling), field mapping, at least 2 test cases, log or screenshot evidence.

practicalTask.title: Record workflow and artefacts (Automatize)
practicalTask.placeholder: Enter or paste your workflow description, field mapping and test case summary…
practicalTask.template: Trigger: new form submission. Actions: (1) Write to Sheets/CRM with field mapping, (2) send personalised email, (3) notify team Slack/Teams. Error handling: what to do if API does not respond.
practicalTask.explanation: Artefacts prove the workflow was built and tested.

slides.title: Practice 2: Augment (15 %)
slides.subtitle: Email → AI summary → human approve → send

scenario.narrativeLead: Build workflow with AI summary step and human approval. Record prompt and rules.
scenario.situation: Augment (15 %): human decides, AI helps – e.g. email → summary → approval before send.
scenario.context: Flow: incoming email → AI summary (e.g. ChatGPT or Make AI module) → human approval before send → send.
scenario.data: Task: build workflow and prepare artefacts – diagram, summary prompt template, approval step description, 1–2 test cases.
scenario.constraints: Use Make or Zapier with AI module. Clearly describe who sees the summary and who approves.
scenario.expectedFormat: Artefacts: workflow diagram, summary prompt, approval rule, 1–2 test cases.

practicalTask.title: Record augment workflow and artefacts
practicalTask.placeholder: Enter workflow summary, summary prompt and approval rules…
practicalTask.template:
Summary prompt: From this email extract: (1) sender and date, (2) main topic in one sentence, (3) 3–5 bullet points. Human-in-the-loop: before sending human approves or edits.

EVALUATOR PROMPT: Check the summary: (1) tone suitable for client, (2) facts match original, (3) no excess PII. If not – return list of fixes.
practicalTask.explanation: Human approval reduces risk of wrong automatic send.

steps.title: Build workflow: trigger (new email) → AI summary → approval step → send
steps.description: Use Make or Zapier with AI module.
steps.hint: Use Make or Zapier with AI module.
steps.partialSolution: Use Make or Zapier with AI module.

steps.title: Write summary prompt template and approval step description
steps.description: Who sees, who approves, what to do if rejected.
steps.hint: Who sees, who approves, what to do if rejected.
steps.partialSolution: Who sees, who approves, what to do if rejected.

steps.title: Write 1–2 test cases and workflow diagram (1 p.)
steps.description: Happy path + one edge case.

EVALUATOR PROMPT: Check the summary: (1) tone suitable for client, (2) facts match original, (3) no excess PII. If not – return list of fixes.

slides.title: Practice 3: Autonomize (5 %)
slides.subtitle: Feedback → sentiment AI → escalation → ticket/task

scenario.narrativeLead: Build sentiment-based escalation workflow. Define thresholds and what to do in an incident.
scenario.situation: Autonomize (5 %): AI agent with limits – feedback, sentiment, escalation to ticket or task.
scenario.context: Flow (roles): **Classifier** → **Sentiment specialist** → **Escalation coordinator** → ticket. Feedback (CRM/form) → sentiment AI → escalation → ticket (Jira, Trello, Teams).
scenario.data: Task: define thresholds, escalation rules and prepare artefacts – diagram, link to incident playbook.
scenario.constraints: Use Make, n8n or Zapier with AI module. State when to create ticket and which fields to fill.
scenario.expectedFormat: Artefacts: workflow diagram (1 p.), sentiment threshold definition, escalation rules, link to incident playbook (5 steps, see doc §20).

practicalTask.title: Record autonomous workflow and rules
practicalTask.placeholder: Enter sentiment thresholds, escalation rules and workflow summary…
practicalTask.template: Sentiment thresholds: if < 3 stars or negative – create ticket and notify team. Escalation rule: ticket fields “sentiment”, “original_text” (short). Incident playbook: see AUTOMATIZAVIMO_IRANKIAI_VERSLUI §20.
practicalTask.explanation: Clear rules prevent wrong mass escalation.

slides.title: Scenario: Research agent
slides.subtitle: Search + summary + sources

scenario.narrativeLead: Pick a topic and use the prompt below with search and sources.
scenario.situation: Optional scenario: research agent – search, summary and sources in one task.
scenario.context: Task: search + summary + sources. Copy prompt into AI and run with your topic.
scenario.data: Use AI with search enabled. Fill placeholders ([X], [ROLE]) with your topic.
scenario.constraints: If no sources – write “Not found” and why. Do not invent links.
scenario.expectedFormat: Artefact: summary with links or clear “Not found” message.

practicalTask.title: Complete research agent task
practicalTask.placeholder: Paste AI answer summary or your notes on sources…
practicalTask.template: Role: [ROLE]. Task: (1) Search [X], (2) pick 3–5 key sources, (3) write summary in English with links. If not found – write “Not found” and why.
practicalTask.explanation: Prepares agent thinking without a full workflow tool.

steps.title: Copy prompt and paste into AI with search enabled
steps.description: Fill [X] with your topic. Run and review sources.
steps.hint: Fill [X] with your topic. Run and review sources.
steps.partialSolution: Fill [X] with your topic. Run and review sources.

content.scenarioDescription: Task: search + summary + sources. Copy prompt into AI and run with your topic.

scenario.narrativeLead: Pick a topic and use the prompt below with search and sources.

content.template: Role: [ROLE]. Task: (1) Search [X], (2) pick 3–5 key sources, (3) write summary in English with links. If not found – write “Not found” and why.

steps.title: Copy prompt and paste into AI with search enabled
steps.description: Fill [X] with your topic. Run and review sources.

slides.title: Scenario: Coordinator + 2 specialists
slides.subtitle: Multi-agent prompt pipeline without code

scenario.narrativeLead: Start here if you want a quick prompt-only path: run a manual pipeline with 3 separate AI chats and handoff rules.
scenario.situation: Recommended start: multi-agent practice in business language – coordinator, specialist, evaluator – without Zapier/Make.
scenario.context: Choose a topic (weekly sales summary or RFP). Use 3 prompts in separate chats. Record the diagram and 1 test case.
scenario.data: Artefacts: diagram (roles + handoff), 3 prompts, 1 test case (e.g. missing data), Skill package for reuse.
scenario.constraints: No Zapier/Make – only prompt orchestration. HITL before final send.
scenario.expectedFormat: Diagram + 3 copied prompts + test case description + Skill package.

practicalTask.title: Multi-agent prompt pipeline
practicalTask.placeholder: Paste your diagram, 3 prompts, test case summary and Skill package…
practicalTask.template:
COORDINATOR: You are the coordinator. Task: [DESCRIBE]. Split into 2 sub-tasks, assign roles, handoff rules.

SPECIALIST: You are [researcher/writer]. Input: [from coordinator]. Do [X]. Output: [format].

EVALUATOR: Check whether all criteria are answered. If not – return for revision with concrete points.

SKILL PACKAGE:
Name: [e.g. RFP summary pipeline]
When to use: [for which task]
Roles: coordinator, specialist, evaluator
Steps: [1–5]
Limits: what AI does not do / when it asks a human
Check: 1 test case + expected answer
Lesson after run: what to update next time
practicalTask.explanation: A manual pipeline prepares multi-agent thinking without extra platforms; later you can move the same logic into Zapier, Make or n8n.

steps.title: Run the coordinator prompt
steps.description: Get a plan with roles and handoff.

steps.title: Run the specialist prompt
steps.description: Pass the coordinator output as input.

steps.title: Run the evaluator prompt
steps.description: Check and record 1 test case.

steps.title: Write the Skill package
steps.description: Combine 3 prompts into one reusable description: name, when to use, roles, steps, limits, check and lesson.

content.scenarioDescription: 3 CopyButton prompts: coordinator, specialist, evaluator. Run manual pipeline. Artefacts: diagram + 3 prompts + 1 test case + Skill package.
content.template:
COORDINATOR: You are the coordinator. Task: [DESCRIBE]. Break into 2 sub-tasks, assign roles, handoff rules.

SPECIALIST: You are [researcher/writer]. Input: [from coordinator]. Do [X]. Output: [format].

EVALUATOR: Check all criteria are met. If not – return for revision with specific points.

SKILL PACKAGE:
Name: [e.g. RFP summary pipeline]
When to use: [for which task]
Roles: coordinator, specialist, evaluator
Steps: [1–5]
Limits: what AI does not do / when it asks a human
Check: 1 test case + expected answer
Lesson after run: what to update next time

slides.title: Optional: recap M10
slides.subtitle: Reports, tools, error handling – pick one

content.whyBenefit: These 3 prompts recap Module 10 basics in a project context. Pick one that matters most to you – you do not need to do all of them.

sections.heading: Report generator
sections.body: META + INPUT + OUTPUT – several steps: data → analysis → 1-page report.
sections.copyable: META: [ROLE]. INPUT: [DATA]. OUTPUT: 1 page, 3–5 bullet points, English.

sections.heading: Tool usage
sections.body: Enable search or calculator on the platform and check whether AI used the tool in its answer.
sections.copyable: Use [tool – search or calculator] and provide result with short explanation. Task: [DESCRIBE].

sections.heading: Error handling and limits
sections.body: Add this to a system prompt and try a task without data – AI must clearly say when it cannot complete the task.
sections.copyable: If the task cannot be completed: write “Failed: [reason]” and suggest what the user can fix. Never return an empty answer.

slides.title: Project summary
slides.subtitle: What next?

content.introBody: Congratulations! You completed the Agent path project: a prompt-only multi-agent start or the full 3-practice delivery-first path. You have a workflow diagram, prompts, a test case, Skill package and templates for further work.

sections.heading: Three practices and 3A
items: Automatize – routine steps without manual work
Augment – AI as helper with approval
Autonomize – limited autonomy cycles with safeguards
Workflow diagram, trigger → action → webhook – as in Module 10

sections.heading: Prompt-only and Skill package
items: Coordinator + 2 specialists – quick start without code
Skill package – name, when to use, roles, steps, limits
System prompt with limits and clear “Failed” messages

sections.heading: Lesson and update
items: After a run, write what failed and why
Update a rule, prompt, skill or test
Repeat with the same edge case
Apply the chosen scenario within 24–48 hours

content.reflectionPrompt:
META: You are a learning reflection assistant. Goal – consolidate the Agent engineering project results.
INPUT: I just completed Module 12: a prompt-only multi-agent start or the full 3-practice delivery-first path. I have a diagram, prompts, at least 1 test case and a Skill package.
OUTPUT: Ask 3 questions: (1) Which agent or workflow step will I apply in 24 hours? (2) Which lesson do I record after the first run? (3) What will I update: rule, prompt, skill or test? After my answers, give one concrete tip.
content.tagline: 3 practices + artefacts + lesson = a result that improves next time.
content.nextStepCTA: Go to Module 13: Content engineering with AI
content.firstAction24h: Within 24–48 hours, repeat the 124.5 prompt-only pipeline with a real process or launch one workflow in your project (Zapier / Make / AI with tools).
